source: All-In Podcast — E274: SpaceX $2T case, Nvidia shock selloff
summary: The Strait of Hormuz closure is relatively good for America and Trump's policy goals due to US energy self-sufficiency, and is a forcing function for reindustrialization
summary_extended: Baker argues that a Strait of Hormuz closure is net negative for the global economy but relatively advantageous for the United States compared to its geopolitical rivals. His reasoning rests on US energy self-sufficiency: the US runs on domestic natural gas for electricity generation and is the world's largest oil and gas exporter, meaning it is insulated from the supply shock that would devastate energy-import-dependent Europe and Asia. He cites specific data to support the asymmetry: domestic natural gas futures (NG1) are down year-to-date while LNG prices for the rest of the world have surged 100-200%, illustrating the divergence in energy cost burdens. The investment implication is that every day the Strait remains closed accelerates US reindustrialization relative to competitors, as higher energy costs abroad erode the manufacturing competitiveness of Europe and Asia. Baker frames this as a structural, if uncomfortable, tailwind for Trump's policy goal of bringing industrial production back to the United States.
detail: Baker argues the Strait of Hormuz closure is bad for everyone but relatively best for America: US is self-sufficient in energy (natural gas powers electricity), is the world's largest oil and gas exporter, and domestic natural gas (NG1) is down YTD while LNG for the rest of the world is up 100-200%. Every day the Strait is closed is relatively good for US reindustrialization and relatively terrible for Europe and Asia.
confidence_evidence: Baker states this as a clear analytical view with specific data points (NG1 down YTD, LNG up 100-200%)
quote_fragment: every day the Strait of Hormuz is closed is relatively good for America
